spoken: This is a web application. We're going to show you what our system sees when it looks at it.
The sieve filtered out the noise and found seven meaningful elements. Classified in under a second. No screenshots sent to an LLM. No tokens burned. Deterministic.
Typable. Typable. Clickable. Chrome. Skip. A human can classify an entire page in under a minute.
Here's the full classification. Every element named, located, typed. This becomes the glossary — the vocabulary that agents and tests are bound to.
Now watch what happens when an agent connects through our MCP server. It doesn't see HTML. It sees the glossary. Login.email is a typable field. Login.submit is a clickable button.
Try to reference something that isn't in the glossary? The system rejects it. Unknown object, strict mode.
Let's add a feature. The agent writes three lines of code to add a Remember Me checkbox to the login form.
Now let's see what the sieve thinks about the new version.
The sieve found a new element. One checkbox that wasn't there before. Everything else is unchanged.
The agent writes a test using validated glossary names. Every element reference is checked at planning time, before it even runs.
Green. The test passes. The feature works.
Now watch what happens when someone breaks something. The checkbox is removed from the code.
The sieve ran again. Login.remember-me is gone. Glossary diff: minus one entry.
The test fails. Not because of a flaky selector. The glossary says Login.remember-me should exist. The sieve says it doesn't. Misfit detected.
The sieve does for E2E testing what unit test frameworks did for functions — makes it so cheap and structured that there's no excuse not to do it.
